coredata.dc:title: Flyback converter employed non-dissipative cell equalization in electric vehicle lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:publicationName: e-Prime - Advances in Electrical Engineering, Electronics and Energy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Selvaraj, Vedhanayaki
Vairavasundaram, Indragandhi
coredata.dc:description: The effective and dependable usage of rechargeable batteries has emerged as a central topic for automobile manufacturers in the wake of the rise of electric vehicle technology. When it comes to rechargeable batteries with high specific energy and specific power, lithium-ion battery technology is the most well-known. The low terminal voltage battery cells in the lithium-ion battery pack are linked in series to provide the necessary voltage for the electric vehicle system. The low charge cell in the string limits the usable capacity of the battery pack, though. Disturbances in the battery pack's charge are due to variations in manufacturing quality and to the unique operating circumstances of each individual cell. These inconsistencies cause a decline in usable capacity, a quickening of cell deterioration, and, most significantly, substantial safety issues including overcharging. The cell balancing controller is a critical component of the battery management system in all electric vehicle and hence performs a crucial function in extending battery life and ensuring the battery's safety. This paper presents a hardware-in-the-loop simulation of a RCD buffer included fly-back converter-based active cell equaliser for lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles. With the equaliser, all the series-connected cells may be brought to a more even State of Charge. All of the MOSFETs employed in the proposed approach are selected for their low conduction loss. The suggested equaliser is able to produce equalization without the need for a switch driving circuit or sophisticated control method, allowing it to function automatically. The system's overall price is reduced, and the balanced circuit's complexity is considerably reduced. In-depth discussions on circuit configuration, operating principle, modeling, and design consideration are presented. Finally, both Matlab simulation results and Hardware-in-loop based experimental findings are offered to back up the claims that the suggested cell equaliser is both practical and effective.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Battery electric vehicle
Active cell balancing
Lithium-ion battery
State of charge
Flyback converter

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: e-Prime - Advances in Electrical Engineering, Electronics and Energy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Non-dissipative flyback converter based cell equalization topology

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SOC comparison of cells without balancing, with active and passive balancing.
Classification of cell balancing topology.
Internal resistance vs SOC curve during charging and discharging at different C-rate [39].
Internal resistance curve during charging and discharging [39].
Algorithm for selection of turn's ratio.
Proposed active cell balancing topology with a flyback converter.
Flowchart of balance control in proposed methodology.
Nominal Current Discharge Characteristic at 0.43478C.
SOC of series-connected cells after active balancing using flyback converter.
SOC of B1 during active balancing using flyback converter.
Voltage of B1 during active balancing using flyback converter.
Current of B1 during active balancing using flyback converter.
SOC of B2 during active balancing using flyback converter.
Voltage of B2 during active balancing using flyback converter.
Current of B2 during active balancing using flyback converter.
SOC of B3 during active balancing using flyback converter.
Voltage of B3 during active balancing using flyback converter.
Current of B3 during active balancing using flyback converter.
HIL setup of active balancing using flyback converter.
HIL simulation of three balanced series connected cells.
HIL simulation of three unbalanced, balanced series connected cells balanced using the proposed technology.
HIL simulation of three unbalanced, balanced series connected cells.
Comparison of conventional active cell balancing topology.

row.entry: Large size, high cost, and complex control logic.
Reduced equalization time as well as a user-friendly modular layout
Due to the high price tag and complexity of the controls, intelligent regulation is essential.
Low switch voltage/current stress, high power handling capacity, low balancing currents, and relative efficiency.
Controlling complexity is significant but implementation is simple.
Some of the advantages of this technology are high power applications, simple modularization, great efficiency, and low switching voltage/current stress.
High degree of control and management.
Module-compatible electric vehicle and hybrid electric vehicle applications.
Low balancing speed, continuous heat dissipation
More cost, efficiency is low and suitable for low power applications
The switch voltage is not overloaded, there is no need for a feedback loop, and the system can handle low-power as well as high-power activities with ease.
Problems with modularity, significant switch voltage stress, and a high overall complexity.
Low-balancing-capacity currents, high-power applications, and straightforward modularization.
Overall slowness and a heavy stress on the switch current.
This device has the ability to manage high power applications, a low switching voltage stress, and a high conversion rate.
Slow progress towards equilibrium; increasing complexity.
Reduced complexity in handling. Need of accurate voltage detection. Fast rate of equalization. High efficiency
Capacitors that act as filters are necessary when working with significant amounts of switching current. Operates only in charging mode.
Relatively low current/voltage stress and has complex control
Low balancing speed, less efficient, cost is high, size is the big, complex control logic
Good balancing speed, good modular design
less efficient, large size, expensive, low balancing speed
High efficiency, high power applications, minimal switch voltage/current stress, and easy installation.
High degree of complexity in operations

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Impact on cell balancing due to internal parameters.

row.entry: Changes take place according to operating modes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Parameters for simulation.
Comparison of proposed cell balancing topology with existing topology based on flyback converter.

row.entry: • Hysteresis logic control to determine the operating mode and presence of current-sensing circuits increases the complexity of proposed methodology. Absence of snubber circuit increases the loss.
• Presence of n number of secondary winding makes the circuit look bulky and consumes more space
• The proposed model is not highly preferred in EV application because of the presence of auxiliary battery that increases the size and cost of proposed methodology

• The major drawback of the proposed method is the limitation on number of cells to be balanced.
• Presence of occasional peak current leads to inconsistency in the battery pack.
Proposed flyback converter with buffer circuit

• SOC based balancing eliminates the inconsistency due to voltage based balancing.
• Presence of RCD buffer eliminates the inductive leakage current and hence reduces the loss
• HIL simulation of the proposed methodology proves it effectiveness in real-time
• Since the enhancement of the system is easy it can be extended for any number of cells.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SOC values before and after cell balancing.
Comparison of proposed cell balancing topology with conventional topology [45].

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: E- Excellent, VG-Very Good, G-Good, HHigh, M-Medium, l-Low, S-Satisfactory.

head.ce:title: Flyback converter employed non-dissipative cell equalization in electric vehicle lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electrical Engineering, VIT University, Vellore, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electrical Engineering, VIT University, Vellore, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effective and dependable usage of rechargeable batteries has emerged as a central topic for automobile manufacturers in the wake of the rise of electric vehicle technology. When it comes to rechargeable batteries with high specific energy and specific power, lithium-ion battery technology is the most well-known. The low terminal voltage battery cells in the lithium-ion battery pack are linked in series to provide the necessary voltage for the electric vehicle system. The low charge cell in the string limits the usable capacity of the battery pack, though. Disturbances in the battery pack's charge are due to variations in manufacturing quality and to the unique operating circumstances of each individual cell. These inconsistencies cause a decline in usable capacity, a quickening of cell deterioration, and, most significantly, substantial safety issues including overcharging. The cell balancing controller is a critical component of the battery management system in all electric vehicle and hence performs a crucial function in extending battery life and ensuring the battery's safety. This paper presents a hardware-in-the-loop simulation of a RCD buffer included fly-back converter-based active cell equaliser for lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles. With the equaliser, all the series-connected cells may be brought to a more even State of Charge. All of the MOSFETs employed in the proposed approach are selected for their low conduction loss. The suggested equaliser is able to produce equalization without the need for a switch driving circuit or sophisticated control method, allowing it to function automatically. The system's overall price is reduced, and the balanced circuit's complexity is considerably reduced. In-depth discussions on circuit configuration, operating principle, modeling, and design consideration are presented. Finally, both Matlab simulation results and Hardware-in-loop based experimental findings are offered to back up the claims that the suggested cell equaliser is both practical and effective.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries typically receive preference over other types of batteries because of their numerous benefits. They include the battery's high power density, long service life, high energy density, lack of memory effect and minimal self-discharge competency [3,4]. These Li-ion batteries, however, are highly temperature-dependent in terms of performance, lifespan, and security. Batteries are sensitive to their surroundings and give out heat through a series of chemical processes when being charged and discharged, therefore temperature fluctuations are common [5]. Many researchers have proposed numerous battery thermal management techniques among which PCM based cooling seems to be highly effective [6].

Since a single Li-ion cell cannot provide the necessary energy, thousands of Li-ion cells are linked in series as well as in parallel to form an EV's battery pack. [7]. Due to the restrictions in manufacturing, unstable work conditions, and self-discharge, Li-ion cells suffer some irregularities in charge and voltage, which reduces the battery's lifecycle. Many types of research are going on in Li-ion batteries to increase their lifetime and enhance their total energy storage capacity. Because of chemical composition, each cell's charge and discharge cycle in a battery pack differs, leading to charge imbalance [8]. Overcharging of a cell leads to an explosion, and undercharging of a cell reduces the battery's lifetime. Therefore, the overall storage capacity of the battery pack is diminished, and cell degradation occurs, under the charge imbalance state. The primary purpose of the Battery Management System (BMS) is to balance cell voltage and monitor the SOC of the battery.

Cell equalization or cell balancing is essential in batteries to protect the cell from degradation, keep the SOC within a safe operating limit, and to enhance battery lifespan [9]. In order to reach a desired high voltage level, it is most common for the individual cells that collectively make up the battery pack to be connected in series. Compared to parallelly connected battery packs, inconsistency is high in series-connected battery packs due to internal resistance, thermal effects, self-discharge rate, and manufacturing defects [10]. Cell balancing may be broken down into two distinct categories according to the balancing circuit, namely the dissipation method or passive balancing and the non-dissipation method or active balancing. Passive balancing dissipates the extra energy using a bleeding resistor or other energy-consuming devices. The advantage of the passive balancing method is its low cost and compact size. Dissipation of energy increases the heat in the circuit, which leads to a thermal imbalance in the cells of battery pack. Hence dissipation method is not highly preferred in EV applications [11]. In non-dissipative balancing strategy, energy is switched from one cell to another using switches, and energy storage components are used to store excess energy. The energy storage components used in active balancing include inductors, transformers, and capacitors. Among all active cell balancing topologies, transformer-based solutions have more advantages because of their easy isolation, high efficiency, and simple implementation [12]. The comparison of active and passive balancing of series-connected cells is given in Fig. 1 .

ce:list-item.ce:para: The major contribution of the proposed work is
Implementation of SOC based active cell balancing using RCD buffer included flyback converter with 1:1 ratio transformer
Reduction of balancing time thereby increasing the balancing speed.
Implementation of the proposed circuit using MOSFET switch to reduce conduction loss
To reduce primary leakage inductance current using RCD buffer circuit
Reducing the complexity of control logic by replacing PWM generators with microcontroller coding-based switching pulses to MOSFETs.
To implement and execute the model in both MATLAB Simulink and HIL using OPAL RT −5700

ce:section.ce:para: Following the introduction section, the literature review for the proposed work is carried out in Section 2. The term cell imbalance and factors affecting it is explained in Section 3. Section 4 discuss about the proposed flyback converter based cell balancing followed by simulation and experimental results in Section 5. The overall summary of the proposed systems is stated in Section 6 and conclusion is provided in Section 7.

Based on energy transfer paths, active cell balancing methods have been classified into six basic categories, namely pack to cell (P2C) [13], cell to pack (C2P) [14], direct cell to cell (DC2C) [15], adjacent cell to cell (AC2C) [16], any cell to any cell (AC2AC) [17], and multicell- to-multicell (MC2MC) [18] methods.
•
In the P2C strategy, energy is transferred from pack to low voltage cell, and in C2P, energy transfers occur from high voltage cell to string.
•
MC2MC method transfers the high voltage string energy to the low voltage string. The above-said methods have a good balancing speed, but inefficient balancing occurs when direct transfer of energy between imbalanced cells cannot be managed.
•
In the AC2C method, energy transformation occurs between two adjacent cells.
•
Direct energy transfer between high-voltage and low-voltage cells occurs by the DC2C method with high transformation efficiency. But in DC2C, complicated control scheme regulates the transfer of energy between two cells.

When the conventional techniques for balancing is examined, it is found that they have specific restrictions in the way energy gets transmitted among the cells. Hence numerous converter based cell balancing topology has been proposed. The classification of cell-equalization topologies based on converters is given in Fig. 2 .

In converter-based topology, the balancing of uneven cells is carried out by applying converters. Though converter topology has high energy transfer efficiency between the cells, they suffer the disadvantage of a complex control algorithm because of passive components and active switches [19,20]. The basic converter topologies available for cell balancing include CUK, buck-boost, boost, quasi quadrant, push-pull, and full-bridge converter. For flexible management of the SOC of a cell, converters control the power flow in any direction as per the commands given by BMS. In [21] , Buck-boost and Cuk converters share energy between cells. In [23], a flyback converter with an ideal transformer achieves cell balancing. In [22], Full-bridge converters transmit energy between the pack and the weakest cell. Zero quasi-resonant converters are also used for cell balancing [24]. The comparison of various cell-balancing topologies is given in Table 1
.

A cascaded H bridge converter-based cell balancing topology was proposed in the paper [25]. The major drawback of H bridge converter-based topology is that it requires a separate dc source for each H Bridge. Further, the circuit complexity also increases by the multi-winding transformer, diode and rectifier requirement. In delta connected Cascaded H bridge converter, magnetic elements are required to reduce the circulating current present in the delta. The complexity of the circuit increases with an increase in number of cells [26]. Modular multilevel converter-based cell balancing is highly preferred in the circuit where individual monitoring and control of each cell is required, mostly in grid-connected systems. MMC-based balancing suffers from single-cell failure; hence, to avail self-healing, it requires additional MOSFET to be connected in series with each cell and parallel with each module. Switching loss will increase with increased switches that reduce the switching efficiency. The major drawback is it has high current and voltage stress, is bulky in size and is expensive due to the increased number of switches [27]. Further, to attain SOC balancing, varying the reference voltage of each cell has to be done, leading to distortion in the output voltage waveform. Combining DC-AC conversion, isolation transformer, and DC-AC conversion is necessary since the operating state's redundancy leads to the unbalanced cell voltage. Machine learning algorithm-based cell balancing was proposed in [28]. The algorithm-based cell balancing reduces the circuit complexity and cost, but it requires huge historical data to train the model. The collection of huge amounts of data makes the process time-consuming due to long-lasting tests and requires expensive test equipment. Transformer based non-dissipative cell equalization was proposed in [29–31]. Though the proposed scheme has high accuracy in balancing, they are not preferred highly due to large size, presence of high leakage inductance and difficulty in expansion of balancing topology.

Since flyback converters are highly preferred in EV application, this paper proposes a flyback converter-based balancing topology. The flyback converter was chosen because it has the following distinguishing characteristics: the feedback circuit is removed, simplifying the design; good efficiency for this class of converters; galvanic electrical isolation between the primary and secondary side; good power density; and does not suffer from the current transfer ratio degradation that arises from temperature rise found in low-cost opt couplers; all of which are essential requirements for the on-board electronics of electric vehicles. These benefits have prompted extensive study of this specific flyback converter. This work presents a proposal for a real-time implementation of active cell balancing in lithium-ion batteries that is based on a RCD buffer included flyback converter. The suggested solution appears to be more favourable than AC2C since it is unaffected by cell position, and it is also superior to DC2C because it can balance numerous pairs of cells at once.

Many internal and external sources contribute to cell imbalance. Internal factors include cell capacity variation and internal impedance affected by the environment. External sources include peripheral circuits and thermal differences occurring across the battery packs. The main reason for the degradation of cell cycling is capacity fading and resistance. The algorithms or techniques involved in cell balancing must charge or discharge the cell based on voltage and SOC. It is challenging to identify the cells that need to be charged and discharged since the OCV-SOC graph is flattened for the mid-SOC levels and is influenced by the internal cell resistor. Hence voltage-based cell balancing methods are not highly preferred. Inconsistency of the state of the battery also affects voltage-based cell balancing.

Relation between the static internal resistance of the charged battery and its SOC is shown in the graph in the Fig. 3 . From the figure, it is observed that the charged battery's static internal resistance fluctuates very slightly when its state-of-charge (SOC) increases at a constant temperature.

The curve that depicts the change of internal resistance corresponding to charging and discharging of the 5Ah lithium ion battery is shown in the Fig. 4 . Internal resistance readings have been consistently stable, indicating that the charge-preparation process is in a constant state. The highest percentage of change in resistance is 0.61% under these circumstances.

Many lithium ions are released from the cathode and seep into the electrolyte as the battery's electrochemical activity rises throughout the charging process. There has been a reduction in the battery's internal resistance and its tendency to limit current flow.

Charged batteries have 12.3% less internal resistance than they had before the charging process began. After being recharged, the battery's internal resistance rises and its rate of growth slows, meaning it never quite recovers to its prior level of performance. When the value reaches 21.1 the internal resistance remains constant. Consequently, 1C of steady current is being used to deplete the battery, causing its internal resistance to increase over time. In SOC-based methods, during high/low SOC, the error due to voltage variation of internal resistance can be minimized by reducing the charging and discharging current. Since SOC based method is less affected by the internal resistance and is nearly flat from empty to full, it is the perfect preference for the operation at all times. Impacts that occur in cell balancing due to internal cell parameters are given in Table 2 .

Parameters of specific ASIC play an important role in design procedure since it varies depending upon the manufacturers. Precise input parameters are required for the successful design procedure. In this paper, the mainly focused input parameters include voltage and SOC with their minimum and maximum tolerable range. The factors such as size, weight, price, temperature tolerance, and power density are not under consideration.

The first step in the design process is figuring out the turn's ratio for the transformer. The ratio of primary and secondary turns is known as the turn's ratio. The selection of turn's ratio of the transformer has to be in a way that it compromises the voltage stress between primary and secondary winding over the MOSFET. Initial step to calculate the turn's ratio is to consider the minimum drain source voltage of the MOSFET from the catalog data. Since the design of MOSFET includes the 15%- 20% safety margin to ensure the safe operation of converter, the drain-source voltage (VDS) can be given as
V
D S
=
V
D S A P
− 15 %
V
D S A P

When the switch S1 is turned off, the drain-source voltage (VDS) will be the sum of reflected output voltage (VREF) and input DC voltage (VDC)
(1)
V
D S
=
(
V

D S 0
)

The turn ratio of the transformer is one of the variables that may be used to compute the reflected output voltage.
(2)
V
R E F
=
(
N S
N P
)
(
V
O U T
)

Here Ns & Np are the number of windings in the primary and secondary winding, respectively. Vout is the output battery voltage, and VDS0 is the overvoltage due to leakage inductance. Based on the nominal drain-source voltage of MOSFET used, the transformer's turn ratio is estimated. The algorithm for calculating the transformers' turn ratio is shown in Fig. 5 .

The oscillation process and the overvoltage depend on several parameters, including leakage inductance on the primary and secondary sides of the transformer and MOSFET output capacitance which cannot be determined more precisely [40,41]. Overvoltage is an important factor since fast charging is a heavy load. To analyze, let us consider the operation of the converter in two modes, namely, charging mode and discharging mode.

The voltage across the primary inductance in the discharging mode with the main switch on is given by

The transformer core is made up of ferrite material and must be selected based on output power. Based on the product of the window area available for winding and cross-sectional area, the transformer core can be selected.

3

The RCD buffer circuit module is a resistor, capacitor, and diode linked in parallel at both ends of the primary coil of the flyback converter. Turning off the primary MOSFET allows the capacitor to discharge the energy held in the converter's leakage inductance into the resistor, relieving the MOSFET's voltage stress. The capacitor will charge more quickly, using more energy from the leakage inductance, if R C is too small. Resistance will use up the primary magnetizing inductance's energy before the MOSFET on the primary side can be turned on. Conduction on the secondary side of the converter will be slowed down if R C is too high, and the resistor-capacitor circuit will use up some of the energy provided by the main excitation inductance. The value of buffer resistance is calculated as the ratio of maximum capacitor voltage to the power in the buffer resistance.
R buffer
=
(
V
C
max
)
2
(
P
R
buffer
)

The value of buffer capacitance can be obtained using the expression
C
b u f f e r

1
Δ
V C

×
f s

Maximum voltage across the buffer diode can be given as
V
D
buffer
≈ 1.2 ×
V
DS _ MAX

ce:section.ce:section-title: Non-dissipative flyback converter based cell equalization topology
ce:section.ce:para: This section delves into the details of the innovative cell balancing technology that has been proposed. Fig. 6 is a schematic representation of the active cell balancing topology that has been suggested. It is impossible to implement the topology's equalization function without the control strategy. Fig. 7 is a flowchart depicting the balancing control system.

The proposed methodology includes 2n MOSFET switches, a buffer circuit consisting of a diode connected in series with a shunt-connected resistor and capacitor and a 1:1 ratio flyback transformer. The cell count in a battery pack is called B1, B2, and B3….Bn. the switch SUn is hooked up to the flyback transformer's primary side. In contrast, all remaining MOSFETs from SU1 to SUn-1 and SL1 to SLn are hooked up to the flyback transformer's secondary side. When the switches on the secondary side are switched off, the voltage stress on the primary switch will be very high and match battery pack voltage. The gate pulse to the switches is given through Microcontroller. The buffer circuit dissipates converter leakage inductance. Series-connected cell terminal voltages make up the battery pack voltage.

The methodology of proposed algorithm is illustrated using following steps:
v
Initially, SOC of each cell during the charging and discharging process of battery pack is estimated by BMS using appropriate SOC estimation algorithm.
v
The balancing algorithm starts to operate by investigating whether the SOC difference occurs among the cells in a battery pack.
v
The proposed cell's overarching goal is to achieve cell balancing by transferring power from the cell with the maximum SOC to the cell with a minimal SOC by means of flyback transformer.
v
During charging, the primary side MOSFET is turned on. The battery pack charges the primary transformer. Hence the current in the primary side inductor increases linearly. During this condition, the polarity of the primary side inductor will be positive up and negative down. Once the charging process gets over, the primary side switch gets turned off. The leakage inductance present in the primary side inductor is observed by the capacitor present in the buffer circuit and dissipated by the resistor.
v
During discharging, the polarity of the primary inductor gets reversed. Hence now the secondary side inductor attains positive up and negative down polarity. The emf induced by the primary side of the transformer is coupled to the secondary side, thereby charging the lowest SOC cell.
v
The overall battery pack charge is used to charge the lowest SOC cell during this process. The algorithm of the proposed method is made so that the corresponding switches of the lowest SOC cell are turned on until the difference between the SOC of cells becomes zero.
v
Since 1:1 ratio transformer is chosen the charge is completely transferred among the cells and hence reduces the effect of magnetic core saturation.
v
The balancing process gets repeated until the SOC difference among the cell is less than 0.1%

The parameters of the battery considered for simulation are shown in Table 3 . The Nominal Current Discharge Characteristic at 0.43478C of the considered battery cells was plotted in Fig. 6. According to Fig. 8 , three primary changes occur on a battery's voltage curve during charging and discharging: at initial stage of discharge from full battery capacity, an exponential drop is observed, during the typical operation of the battery, around the rated voltage a linear section is observed which act as nominal area, and when the battery reaches nearly discharge rate, a nonlinear plot is observed.

To assess the effectiveness of the suggested non-dissipative balancing topology, three battery cells are connected in series with two corresponding MOSFET switches. A 1:1 flyback transformer is considered in the proposed method. The SOC of B1, B2 and B3 cells considered for simulation is 50, 30, and 70 with a nominal voltage of 4.2 V and nominal capacity of 5.4 Ah. The balancing of 3 cells using the proposed method is shown in Fig. 9 . Since the B3 cell has the highest SOC compared to B2 and B1, the energy from the highest SOC cell, B3, is transferred to the lowest SOC cells. Cell B3 and B1 have less SOC difference; hence, they get balanced priory at 92 s and attain 55% SOC. Cell B2 also receives energy and attains 35% SOC during this time. Later, once B1 and B3 are balanced, they start to balance B2 together. B2 is balanced with B1 and B3 at 1244 s at 44.5% SOC.

The voltage and current characteristics of the B1 cell and its SOC plot are shown in Figs. 10 , 11 and 12
. From the graph, it is analyzed that till 92 s, cell B1 receives the energy. During this process, the cell's voltage attains up to 5 V and the current decreases to −60 A. Once the battery B3 balances B1 and both start to balance B2, the voltage of B1 decreases to 4.45 V, and the current of B1 attains 10A since the B1 is in supplying mode. After 1244 s, a slight increase in SOC is observed, and hence the voltage of the cell increase to 4.5 V and the current reduces to 5A.

Figs. 11, 12, and 13 display the voltage and current characteristics of the B2 cell as well as its SOC plot. It is deduced from the graph that cell B2 receives the energy for 92 s. The cell's voltage reaches 4.8 V throughout this procedure, while the current drops to −10 A. The voltage of battery B1 rises to 5 V, and the current of battery B2 reaches −60A once battery B3 balances battery B1, and both begin to balance battery B2. Three battery cells are balanced after 1244 s, and because the battery is discharging, the cell's voltage decreases, and the current rises to 2.5 A (Figs. 14 , 15 ).

Figs. 16 , 17 , and 18 depict the B3 cell's voltage and current characteristics and the SOC plot. The graph reveals that cell B3 continues to supply energy for 92 s. During this phase, the cell's voltage reaches 3.9 V and its current increases to 60 A. B3′s voltage rises to 4.5 V, and B3′s current reach 10A once battery B3 balances B1 and both batteries begin to balance B2. Three battery cells are balanced after 1244 s, and because the battery is receiving energy, its voltage increases slightly, and its current rises to 5 A.

The proposed non-dissipative cell equalization utilizing flyback converter methodology is executed and verified using OPALRT-5700. Fig. 19 depicts the hardware configuration of flyback converter-based non-dissipative cell equalization. It involves a host computer, real-time simulator and 4-scope DSO.

The proposed methodology is initially set up with 50% SOC for the three series connected battery cells (B1, B2 and B3). The model is simulated with 50 s/div and a delay of 249 s. The plot obtained is shown in Fig. 20 . From the graph, it is observed that the plot of balanced cells coincides with each other.

Imbalance in the series connected battery cells is created and set as 50 for cell B1, 44 for B2 and 55 for B3. The model is executed with a 50 s/ div time scale and the vertical scale of 25 for B1, 22 for B2 and 27.5 for B3. A delay of 250 s is observed. Fig. 21 shows that the battery cell with 55% and 50% SOC gets balanced at 210 s and cell with 44% SOC gets balanced at 450 s. The three cells are balanced at the SOC of 48.5%.

To analyze the proposed system's effectiveness, the cells' imbalanced conditions are interchanged. B1 cell is provided with 55% SOC, B2 with 50% SOC and B3 with 44% SOC. The model is executed with a 50 s/ div time scale and the vertical scale of 27.5 for B1, 25 for B2 and 22.1 for B3. A delay of 250 s is observed. Fig. 22 shows that the cell with 55% SOC and 50% get balanced at 20 s. A steep decrease in the B1 waveform is observed till 20 s as it is supplying, and in B2 and B3 waveforms steep increase is found due to receiving. The B3 cell gets energy from B1, and B2 and gets balanced with them at 440 s with 48% SOC.

As indicated in Table 4 , the active cell balancing approach suggested in this literature is compared to other existing balancing topologies utilizing a flyback converter. Table 5 shows the efficiency of the proposed system in MATLAB simulation and a real-time simulator. According to the table, the suggested technique performs extremely well in terms of cost, balancing time, and balancing efficiency.
v
The cost of the model depends on the number of switches, transformers, inductors, capacitors and diodes used in the model. The proposed model involves 2n switches with one buffer circuit only. Since only MOSFET is used as switching device with 1:1 ratio transformer, the cost of proposed system is less compared to conventional flyback converter based topologies.
v
From the Table 4, it is observed that the proposed method takes to attain cell equalization compared to conventional balancing methods.
v
The proposed balancing topology transfers energy from high-capacity cells to low-capacity cells. Since the system complexity is based on the control circuits involved, this method eliminates PWM generator and uses microcontroller based gate pulse to the MOSFETS. Hence the system is highly symmetrical. Expansion of the battery cells is very easy as only the number of switches increases.
v
Using a 1:1 ratio flyback converter reduces the system's size and complexity, making the proposed balancing system highly preferable for electric vehicle applications.

The proposed converter topology is compared with the conventional cell balancing converter topology and is listed in Table 6 . The parameters included in the comparison include speed, power loss, cost, size, voltage, and current, Number of switches, execution, control, balancing component, and efficiency. For the shunting resistance, the equalizer balancing speed is low but requires temperature control. Though multi winding transformer and resonant converter equalizer have good balancing speed and high accuracy, it suffers from high electrical stress, and also the design is complex. Buck-boost converter and shunting capacitor have excellent balancing speed and low speed, but they are costly. The proposed battery equalizer can balance the cell both in charging and discharging mode. Hence it can be preferred in Electric Vehicles with a high rating.

Over time, cyclic charging and discharging will exacerbate existing performance variations within a battery pack, distort the amount of energy stored in each cell, and even cause certain batteries to be overcharged or over discharged. This article presents an alternative approach to battery equalization by proposing a fly-back converter based active cell balancing method for equalization with an RCD buffer with the goals of achieving global uniformity and lowering balancing costs. The following were some of the key benefits to adopting this new approach.
•
Without knowing the capacity and resistance of the cells in advance, an accurate estimate of the SOC of all cells in the pack can be produced. However, conventional SOC monitoring tools like Kalman filtering methods needed precise, real-time data on the capacity of each cell.
•
Utilization of MOSFET switches in the proposed cell equalizer reduces the conduction loss
•
Buffer circuit consisting of a diode connected in series with a shunt-connected resistor and capacitor is included to dissipate the converters leakage inductance.
•
The circuit design is simplified and offers great conversion efficiency when the proposed methodology is implemented with a flyback transformer having a 1:1 ratio. Every individual cell can get energy from a battery pack. The positive aspect of the suggested system is its inexpensiveness, efficient balancing speed, and simple expansion.

The design and the methodology of the proposed work is described in detail. Using precise modeling of the lithium-ion battery and appropriate application of the SOC estimation technique, a prototype for simulation was established in MATLAB-Simulink and OPALRT 5700 module, validating the suggested architecture.

The following are some potential topics for further study:
•
Better SOC estimate by factoring in motor and FET temperatures, ambient temperatures, driving distance, driving style, and battery age along with current, voltage ambient temperature and charging/ discharging cycles.
•
Guarantees of resilience against SOC estimation mistakes in equalization schemes.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr.V.Indra Gandhi completed B.E in Electrical and Electronics Engineering from Bharadhidasan University in the year 2004. She received M.E in Power Electronics and Drives from Anna University and awarded Gold Medal for the achievement of University first rank. Subsequently, she is awarded with doctorate of philosophy and accomplished the thesis for Ph.D. entitled “Analysis and Modeling of Multi-Port DC-DC Boost Converter for Hybrid Power Generation System” at Anna University, Chennai, India. At present, she is serving as a Professor in the School of Electrical Engineering, VIT University, Vellore, Tamil Nadu. She has been engaged in research work for the past 15 years in the area of Power Electronics, Drives and Renewable Energy Systems. Area of interest includes power converters, artificial intelligence, and power quality.

S.Vedhanayaki completed B.E in Electrical and Electronics Engineering from Anna University. She received M.E in power systems from Government College of Technology, Coimbatore. At present, she is serving as a research scholar, in the School of Electrical Engineering, VIT University, Vellore, Tamil Nadu. Area of interest includes power converters, artificial intelligence, and electric vehicle.
